questions.question: What are the main advantages and disadvantages of bus-based interconnection networks? (3 marks)
keyPoints: Advantages: Simple implementation, low cost, easy to add devices.
Disadvantages: Limited bandwidth (only one device at a time), poor scalability, single point of failure, becomes a bottleneck with many processors.
Suitable for small systems (few processors) but not for large parallel computers.

questions.question: Calculate the diameter, node degree, and total number of links in a 5-dimensional hypercube. How many nodes does it have? (3 marks)
keyPoints: Node degree: d = 5 (each node connects to 5 neighbors).

questions.question: Compare and contrast static and dynamic interconnection networks, providing examples of each type and discussing their appropriate applications. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Static networks have fixed wiring: connections cannot change at runtime. Examples: ring, 2D mesh, hypercube.
Dynamic networks use switches that can be reconfigured: paths are established on demand. Examples: crossbar, omega (multistage) network.
Static networks are simpler and cheaper but have fixed communication patterns. Dynamic networks are more flexible but more complex and expensive.
Static networks are common in distributed-memory clusters (mesh, torus). Dynamic networks are used in shared-memory systems (crossbar for memory access) and network switches.
Trade-off: static offers predictable latency with lower cost; dynamic offers flexibility with higher cost.

questions.question: Explain the three switching techniques (circuit switching, packet switching, and wormhole routing) used in interconnection networks. Discuss one advantage and one disadvantage of each. (8 marks)
keyPoints: Circuit switching: establishes a dedicated path before data transfer. Advantage: guaranteed bandwidth and low latency once path is set up. Disadvantage: path setup delay and wasted bandwidth when path is idle.

Packet switching (store-and-forward): data is broken into packets that are independently routed. Advantage: efficient use of network bandwidth and flexible routing. Disadvantage: higher latency because each switch must receive the entire packet before forwarding.

Wormhole routing: message is divided into small flits; the header flit routes through the network and the body follows in a pipeline. Advantage: very low latency (only header needs to be routed fully). Disadvantage: can cause blocking if a channel is occupied, potentially leading to deadlock.
Wormhole routing is the most common technique in modern parallel systems due to its low latency.
